title: 21,31-34. The family of Jesus part 2
scripture_text: “Then Jesus entered a house, and again a crowd gathered, so that he and his disciples were not even able to eat. Then his family heard about this, they went to take charge of him, for they said, “He is out of his mind.” Then Jesus’ mother and brothers arrived. Standing outside, they sent someone in to call him. A crowd was sitting around him, and they told him, “Your mother and brothers are outside looking for you.” “Who are my mother and my brothers?” he asked. Then he looked at those seated in a circle around him and said “Here are my mother and my brothers! Whoever does God’s will is my brother and sister and mother.’’
devotional:
Those who try to control others can sometimes be trying to hide their own insecurities. When Jesus’ family heard what Jesus was doing I wonder did they feel threatened and vulnerable? Or was it that they just didn’t understand what He was doing, so in an attempt to manage their feelings and perceptions, they maybe thought if they could bring Jesus under their control, then they could protect him and prevent him from doing all the things that was concerning the religious authorities. Sometimes the purpose of controlling others can be our way of trying to protect them and at the same time manage how we are feeling.

Those who are trying to control others may be living at a 'distance' from those they want to control. As we have said Jesus' family were living in Nazareth, while Jesus was living in Capernaum about thirty miles away, two days walk. But the distance we have with others may not be geographical, it may be emotional or spiritually. Sometimes when relationships are not fulfilling their emotional expectations, people can take on the role of a controller in order to medicate the sense of loss they are feeling.

Those who want to control others can avoid meaningful communication. When Jesus’ family came looking for him it is interesting what they did not do. They did not wait until he was finished before sending someone else in to get him. There was a sense of panic, they needed to act immediately, and they could not wait. They did not ask him questions or give him a chance to explain himself. They made a number of assumptions, and decided how they would deal with him based on these assumptions.

Those who want to control others can be motivated by second hand knowledge not on facts. We are told, "When they heard about this they went to take charge of him." They did not feel a need to go out and gather the facts for themselves. When our hearts are set on controlling others we will not take the time to gather the facts for ourselves; we will use any excuse to justify wrong perceptions.

Our desire to control others means that we could easily perceive the other person as the problem. They came to Jesus with an attitude that said, "we need to change or fix you" You are the problem not us. This same attitude prevents us from seeing our own problems.

When we feel a need to control others, we build our relationships on merit rather than on grace. The desire that Jesus' family had to take charge of him indicated that for him to retain their acceptance, then he would have to change the way he was behaving.
prayer: Father please show me if I am controlling.
html:
21,31-34. The family of Jesus part 2

Mark 3:20
“Then Jesus entered a house, and again a crowd gathered, so that he and his disciples were not even able to eat. Then his family heard about this, they went to take charge of him, for they said, “He is out of his mind.” Then Jesus’ mother and brothers arrived. Standing outside, they sent someone in to call him. A crowd was sitting around him, and they told him, “Your mother and brothers are outside looking for you.” “Who are my mother and my brothers?” he asked. Then he looked at those seated in a circle around him and said “Here are my mother and my brothers! Whoever does God’s will is my brother and sister and mother.’’

Those who try to control others can sometimes be trying to hide their own insecurities. When Jesus’ family heard what Jesus was doing I wonder did they feel threatened and vulnerable? Or was it that they just didn’t understand what He was doing, so in an attempt to manage their feelings and perceptions, they maybe thought if they could bring Jesus under their control, then they could protect him and prevent him from doing all the things that was concerning the religious authorities. Sometimes the purpose of controlling others can be our way of trying to protect them and at the same time manage how we are feeling.

Those who are trying to control others may be living at a 'distance' from those they want to control. As we have said Jesus' family were living in Nazareth, while Jesus was living in Capernaum about thirty miles away, two days walk. But the distance we have with others may not be geographical, it may be emotional or spiritually. Sometimes when relationships are not fulfilling their emotional expectations, people can take on the role of a controller in order to medicate the sense of loss they are feeling.

Those who want to control others can avoid meaningful communication. When Jesus’ family came looking for him it is interesting what they did not do. They did not wait until he was finished before sending someone else in to get him. There was a sense of panic, they needed to act immediately, and they could not wait. They did not ask him questions or give him a chance to explain himself. They made a number of assumptions, and decided how they would deal with him based on these assumptions.

Those who want to control others can be motivated by second hand knowledge not on facts. We are told, "When they heard about this they went to take charge of him." They did not feel a need to go out and gather the facts for themselves. When our hearts are set on controlling others we will not take the time to gather the facts for ourselves; we will use any excuse to justify wrong perceptions.

Our desire to control others means that we could easily perceive the other person as the problem. They came to Jesus with an attitude that said, "we need to change or fix you" You are the problem not us. This same attitude prevents us from seeing our own problems.

When we feel a need to control others, we build our relationships on merit rather than on grace. The desire that Jesus' family had to take charge of him indicated that for him to retain their acceptance, then he would have to change the way he was behaving.

Prayer: Father please show me if I am controlling.
Bible in a year reading plan: -1Samuel 17:26-19:24
